metadata.subject: English Language Arts
metadata.description: English Studies 12 is the comprehensive Grade 12 ELA course integrating literary study, composition, and oral communication. Students engage with sophisticated literary and informational texts, develop nuanced analytical and expressive writing skills, and build the critical literacy needed for post-secondary education and engaged citizenship. A distinctive feature of this course is its explicit attention to First Peoples texts and to Reconciliation as a context for Canadian literature.
metadata.general_description: A comprehensive senior ELA course integrating literary analysis, sophisticated composition, and oral communication, with explicit attention to First Peoples texts and Canada's responsibilities in relation to Reconciliation.
metadata.provincial_exam_note: English Studies 12 has a provincial examination component.

big_ideas.statement: The exploration of text and story deepens our understanding of diverse, complex ideas about identity, others, and the world.
big_ideas.elaboration: At the senior level, literary study becomes a lens for examining the most fundamental human questions — about belonging, justice, mortality, love, and what it means to live well.

big_ideas.statement: People understand text differently depending on their worldviews and perspectives.
big_ideas.elaboration: Mature readers understand that their own positionality shapes interpretation — and engage with literary disagreement as productive rather than threatening.

big_ideas.statement: Texts are socially, culturally, geographically, and historically constructed.
big_ideas.elaboration: Senior students situate texts within their historical and cultural contexts and analyze how those contexts shape meaning.

big_ideas.statement: Language shapes ideas and influences others.
big_ideas.elaboration: At Grade 12, students analyze language not just for what it says but for how it works — how specific choices create effects, shape identities, and exercise power.

big_ideas.statement: Questioning what we hear, read, and view contributes to our ability to be educated and engaged citizens.
big_ideas.elaboration: Critical literacy at the senior level means analyzing texts for ideology, examining their assumptions about power and society, and developing the tools to resist manipulation.

big_ideas.statement: The examination of First Peoples cultures and lived experiences through text builds understanding of Canadians' responsibilities in relation to Reconciliation.
big_ideas.elaboration: Reading and studying First Peoples texts is not optional or supplementary — it is central to understanding Canada and to fulfilling the responsibilities all Canadians have in relation to Reconciliation.

categories.name: Comprehend and Connect (Reading, Listening, Viewing)
categories.raw: Comprehend and Connect — Reading, Listening, Viewing

competencies.raw: Read for enjoyment and achievement; recognize First Peoples perspectives, diversity, and land/place influence.
i_can_statements: I can engage with literature for both personal pleasure and intellectual challenge.
I can analyze First Peoples texts with full attention to their cultural context, oral tradition, and relationship to land.
I can explain the legal status of First Peoples oral tradition in Canada and its significance.

competencies.raw: Evaluate relevance, accuracy, and reliability of texts; apply comprehension strategies in varied contexts to guide inquiry and transform thinking.
i_can_statements: I can evaluate secondary sources about literature with critical awareness of perspective and bias.
I can apply sophisticated reading strategies to transform my understanding of complex texts.
I can guide a rigorous literary inquiry using self-generated questions.

competencies.raw: Understand and appreciate how different forms and features reflect purposes and messages; think critically and creatively to analyze ideas within, between, and beyond texts.
i_can_statements: I can analyze how a text's form and structure are inseparable from its meaning.
I can synthesize ideas across multiple texts to construct a complex literary argument.
I can examine a text's assumptions, silences, and ideological positions.

competencies.raw: Recognize and analyze personal, social, cultural contexts; appreciate how language constructs identities; construct meaningful personal connections; analyze literary elements; discern nuanced word meanings; identify bias, contradictions, omissions.
i_can_statements: I can analyze how race, gender, class, and colonialism shape both the texts I read and my reading of them.
I can trace the connotative and denotative weight of specific language choices in a sophisticated text.
I can identify what a text omits or silences and explain the ideological significance of those omissions.

categories.name: Create and Communicate (Writing, Speaking, Representing)
categories.raw: Create and Communicate — Writing, Speaking, Representing

competencies.raw: Respectfully exchange ideas from diverse perspectives to build shared understanding and transform thinking.
i_can_statements: I can participate in sophisticated literary dialogue, building on others' ideas and offering my own.
I can represent and engage fairly with interpretations I disagree with.
I can contribute to collective intellectual growth through rigorous discussion.

competencies.raw: Respond to text personally, creatively, and critically; select and apply appropriate speaking and listening skills; demonstrate formal and informal communication.
i_can_statements: I can write literary responses that are analytically sophisticated and personally authentic.
I can communicate ideas clearly and persuasively in formal academic and informal conversational contexts.
I can deliver a polished oral presentation on a literary or cultural topic.

competencies.raw: Use writing and design processes; express and support opinions with evidence to achieve purpose; reflect, assess, and refine texts.
i_can_statements: I can write a sophisticated literary analysis essay with a clear argument, specific evidence, and nuanced commentary.
I can revise my writing for clarity, sophistication, and impact through multiple drafts.
I can produce writing that meets the demands of post-secondary academic contexts.

competencies.raw: Use Canadian spelling, grammar, and punctuation proficiently; use citations; transform ideas to create original texts using varied genres, forms, structures, and styles.
i_can_statements: I can apply conventions of Canadian spelling, grammar, and punctuation consistently and contextually.
I can cite all literary and research sources accurately using MLA or another accepted format.
I can produce original texts — analytical, creative, multimodal — that demonstrate senior-level sophistication.

areas.name: Text Forms and Genres
areas.raw: Narrative, journal, procedural, expository, explanatory, news article, e-mail, blog, advertisements, poetry, novel, letter; adventure, fable, fairy tale, fantasy, folklore, historical, horror, legend, mystery, mythology, science fiction, biography, essay, journalism, memoir, personal narrative, speech; Reconciliation in Canada contexts.
topics: Literary fiction: novel, short story, novella
Poetry: lyric, narrative, spoken word, concrete, experimental
Drama: stage, film, radio
Non-fiction: memoir, essay, literary journalism, biography
First Peoples texts: traditional and contemporary, oral and written
Texts addressing Reconciliation: personal narratives, policy documents, TRC Calls to Action
Multimodal texts: documentary, podcast, graphic novel

areas.name: Text Features and Structures
areas.raw: Visual/graphic text elements; narrative structures in First Peoples texts; protocols related to First Peoples oral text ownership; legal status of First Peoples oral tradition in Canada.
topics: Narrative structures: linear, circular, iterative, non-linear, fragmented
First Peoples narrative structures and protocols for use
The legal recognition of oral tradition (Delgamuukw v. BC, 1997)
Point of view and narrative distance in sophisticated texts
Genre hybridity: texts that blend multiple forms

areas.name: Strategies and Processes
areas.raw: Reading strategies, oral language strategies, metacognitive strategies, writing processes, presentation techniques, multimodal reading strategies.
topics: Close reading and annotation of complex texts
Literary research: using databases, criticism, and scholarly sources
Academic essay writing: thesis, argument, evidence, commentary, counterargument
Provincial exam preparation: timed writing, reading unseen texts, responding under exam conditions
Multimodal analysis: reading film, visual art, and digital texts as literature

areas.name: Language Features, Structures, and Conventions
areas.raw: Elements of style; usage and conventions; citation techniques; literary elements and devices.
topics: Advanced diction: connotation, denotation, etymology, register
Syntax for effect: anaphora, periodic sentences, fragments, inverted syntax
Tone and irony: subtle, extended, and structural irony
Extended metaphor, allegory, and intertextuality
Narrative technique: unreliable narrators, free indirect discourse, stream of consciousness
Formal academic style: thesis, evidence, analysis, transitions
MLA citation: in-text and Works Cited
Reconciliation language: understanding terminology and its significance
